questionText: What is the correct hand hygiene procedure before beginning allergen extract compounding?

options.text: A quick 20-second soap-and-water wash
Alcohol-based hand rub applied to dry hands only
Soap-and-water wash for at least 30 seconds, thoroughly dried, followed by alcohol-based hand sanitizer
Gloves alone are sufficient; no additional hand hygiene is required

explanation: USP 797 requires thorough handwashing (≥30 seconds) followed by alcohol-based sanitizer before gloving. Gloves alone do not substitute for proper hand hygiene.

questionText: What is the correct order for donning PPE before allergen extract compounding?

options.text: Gloves → gown → mask → hair cover
Hair cover → mask/face covering → gown → gloves
Gown → hair cover → gloves → mask
Mask → gloves → gown → hair cover

explanation: Garbing follows head-to-toe order to prevent contamination of cleaner items: hair cover first (controls shedding), then mask, gown, and finally gloves last.

questionText: During gloved fingertip and thumb sampling, which culture medium is used?

options.text: Blood agar plates
Chocolate agar plates
Trypticase soy agar (TSA) plates with neutralizing additives
MacConkey agar plates

explanation: TSA plates with neutralizing additives are required. The neutralizer inactivates residual sanitizer on gloves so it doesn't suppress microbial growth and produce a false-negative result.

questionText: How many times must a NEW compounder pass gloved fingertip/thumb sampling before compounding independently?

options.text: Once
Twice
Three separate occasions
Five times

explanation: USP 797 §21 requires initial qualification on at least three separate occasions to demonstrate consistently aseptic technique before independent compounding.

questionText: What is the purpose of the media fill test?

options.text: To verify the sterility of raw allergen extract components
To confirm refrigerator performance
To evaluate the compounder's aseptic technique by simulating the compounding process with growth media
To measure the accuracy of dilution calculations

explanation: The media fill test uses microbial growth medium in place of real extract. Any contamination introduced by the compounder's technique will result in visible growth, flagging a problem before any patient vials are prepared.

questionText: Microbial growth is found on a compounder's fingertip sampling plate. What is the correct next step?

options.text: Average the result with prior passing samples — one failure is acceptable
Stop compounding, complete remediation training, and re-evaluate all competency components before resuming
Continue compounding if only 1 of 10 fingers tested positive
Repeat the test immediately on the same day to get a passing result

explanation: Any microbial growth is a failure. The compounder must immediately stop, receive remediation (retraining in hand hygiene and garbing), and pass re-evaluation of all three components before resuming.

questionText: Which surface areas must be sampled during gloved fingertip and thumb sampling?

options.text: Fingertips of the dominant hand only (four fingers)
All fingertips and both thumbs of both gloved hands
Index finger and thumb of the dominant hand only
The palm of each hand

explanation: All ten fingertips and both thumbs must be sampled — the full glove contact surface. Compounding involves both hands, so both must be evaluated.

questionText: Which statement about the compounding environment for allergen extracts is accurate under USP 797 §21?

options.text: Compounding must occur inside a certified ISO 5 laminar airflow hood
Allergen extracts have a specific exception and may be prepared in a designated low-traffic area without a primary engineering control, if all other §21 requirements are met
A hospital-grade HEPA filter is mandatory for all allergen compounding areas
Compounding may occur anywhere in the office as long as gloves are worn

explanation: USP 797 §21 (the allergen extract exception) specifically permits allergen compounding without a primary engineering control (PEC) if all other requirements — garbing, aseptic technique, SOPs, and competency documentation — are met.

questionText: A vial is mixed January 15, 2026. The earliest component expires September 1, 2026. What BUD should be assigned?

options.text: January 15, 2027 (1 year from mixing)
September 1, 2026 (earliest component expiry)
July 15, 2026 (6 months from mixing)
March 1, 2027 (arbitrary future date)

explanation: BUD = earlier of (1) earliest component expiry or (2) 1 year from mixing. September 1, 2026 comes before January 15, 2027, so September 1, 2026 is the correct BUD.

questionText: A vial has passed its beyond-use date. What is the correct action?

options.text: Use it if the extract appears clear and unchanged
Extend the BUD by 30 days and document the extension
Discard the vial immediately and notify the prescribing physician for a replacement set
Administer remaining doses at a reduced volume to compensate for reduced potency

explanation: A vial past its BUD must be discarded without exception. Visual clarity does not indicate potency or sterility. Administering an expired vial is a patient safety violation.

questionText: Under USP 797 §21, how must refrigerator temperature be monitored for allergen extract storage?

options.text: A weekly manual check is sufficient
Monthly checks documented in a log
Continuous monitoring records must be maintained — gaps are not acceptable
Temperature monitoring is only required for CLIA-certified laboratory refrigerators

explanation: USP 797 requires continuous temperature monitoring with documented records. Any gap in the log means there is no proof extracts were maintained at safe temperature during that period — a significant compliance failure.

questionText: Which of the following would legitimately extend the beyond-use date of a compounded allergen extract?

options.text: Adding a higher concentration of phenol preservative
Using premium-certified allergen extracts from an accredited manufacturer
Nothing — BUD is fixed by the earliest component expiry and 1-year rule; no practice action can extend it
Storing the vial frozen at −20°C after mixing

explanation: BUDs under USP 797 §21 are fixed limits. No additive, storage modification, or practice decision can extend a BUD beyond the earlier of the component expiry or 1 year. Freezing is prohibited as it destroys potency.

questionText: A patient's active vial set will reach its BUD in 3 weeks. What is the appropriate action?

options.text: Continue using the vials until they expire, then stop injections
Notify the patient and prescribing physician now so a new set can be prepared before the BUD
Add fresh diluent to the existing vials to refresh the BUD
Freeze the vials to pause the BUD countdown

explanation: Proactive notification allows the physician to order a replacement set in time to maintain the patient's injection schedule without interruption — essential for immunotherapy effectiveness.

questionText: You need to prepare a 1:100 v/v dilution to fill a 10 mL vial. How much concentrate and diluent are needed?

options.text: 1 mL concentrate + 9 mL diluent
0.1 mL concentrate + 9.9 mL diluent
0.01 mL concentrate + 9.99 mL diluent
5 mL concentrate + 5 mL diluent

explanation: 1:100 v/v = 1 part concentrate per 100 parts total. For 10 mL: 10 ÷ 100 = 0.1 mL concentrate; 10 − 0.1 = 9.9 mL diluent.

questionText: A patient receives 0.5 mL of a 1:100 v/v dilution. What fraction of the full-strength concentrate does this dose represent?

options.text: 1/100 of full strength
1/200 of full strength
1/50 of full strength
1/500 of full strength

explanation: The vial is 1/100 of full strength. Giving 0.5 mL from a 10 mL vial delivers 1/20 of the vial's content. Total: 1/100 × 1/2 = 1/200 of full-strength concentrate.

questionText: Under USP 797 §21, what is the maximum beyond-use date that can be assigned to a compounded allergen vial?

options.text: 6 months from the mixing date
12 months (1 year) from the mixing date
18 months from the mixing date
24 months if refrigerated continuously

explanation: USP 797 §21 sets a hard cap of 1 year (12 months) from compounding as the maximum BUD. No practice action can extend beyond this limit.

questionText: A vial is mixed April 8, 2026. The allergen concentrate expires August 15, 2026; glycerin diluent expires March 1, 2027. What BUD should be assigned?

options.text: April 8, 2027 (1 year from mixing)
August 15, 2026 (earliest component expiry)
March 1, 2027 (diluent expiry)
October 8, 2026 (arbitrary 6-month policy)

explanation: The BUD must be the EARLIER of (1) earliest component expiry or (2) 1 year from mixing. August 15, 2026 is earlier than April 8, 2027, so it is the correct BUD.

questionText: Which statement about allergen immunotherapy vial color coding is correct?

options.text: Color coding is optional; any labeling system the practice designs is acceptable
AAAAI and ACAAI recommend standardized color coding to reduce concentration mix-up errors
Color coding is only required for the most concentrated (maintenance) vial
Color coding applies only to pediatric patients

explanation: AAAAI/ACAAI standardized vial color coding is designed to prevent dangerous mix-ups between dilution concentrations. Published research shows fewer than 25% of practices include all five required label elements; color code is among the most frequently missing.

questionText: When a patient misses several weeks of injections and requires a dose reduction, which document governs how much to reduce the dose?

options.text: The mixing compounder's clinical judgment
The supervising physician's written dose-reduction protocol in the immunotherapy prescription
A pharmacist recommendation
The patient's stated preference

explanation: All dose adjustments must follow the physician's written protocol. Deviating without a written order is both a billing compliance issue (CMS) and a patient safety risk.

questionText: Which items must be recorded in the compounding record for each allergen extract vial prepared?

options.text: Patient name only
Patient name, allergen content, dilution concentrations, volumes, compounder identity, verifier identity, lot numbers of all components, and assigned BUD
Compounder name and date of mixing only
Prescribing physician name only

explanation: USP 797 §21 requires a complete compounding record supporting full chain-of-custody: who mixed it, what went in, at what concentrations, from which lots, who verified it, and when it expires.

questionText: What must be documented for each allergen immunotherapy injection encounter?

options.text: The vial set used and volume administered only
Date/time of injection, vial used, volume, injection site, time observation ended, 20-minute site reaction, any systemic reaction, and the administering staff member's identity
Administering staff signature only
Only systemic reactions; local reactions do not require documentation

explanation: Complete per-injection documentation is required by HIPAA, Medicare CMS LCD L36408, and patient safety standards. Missing the 20-minute observation entry is the most common Medicare audit failure in allergy practices.

questionText: How long must a patient be observed after receiving an allergen immunotherapy injection?

options.text: 15 minutes
20 minutes
30 minutes
60 minutes

explanation: AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters require a minimum 30-minute observation period. Most serious systemic reactions occur within 20–30 minutes; the 30-minute window provides a safety margin.

questionText: Which statement correctly describes the annual competency requirement under USP 797 §21?

options.text: Passing the AAAAI online quiz alone satisfies all annual USP 797 requirements
Annual competency requires three components: written quiz, gloved fingertip/thumb sampling, and a media fill test
Only the media fill test is needed for experienced compounders
Staff with 3+ years of experience are exempt from annual testing

explanation: All three components are required annually for every active compounder regardless of experience. The AAAAI quiz satisfies the written component only — it does not replace the physical glove sampling or media fill test.

questionText: How long should staff competency records (quiz scores, glove sampling, media fill results) be retained?

options.text: 1 year after the staff member departs
Per the practice's document retention policy, typically a minimum of 3–7 years, even after the staff member leaves
6 months after each annual assessment
Records may be destroyed upon staff departure

explanation: Competency records may be needed in regulatory audits or litigation years after the fact. Retain per document retention policy, minimum 3–7 years (longer in states with extended statutes of limitations).

questionText: Which medication must be immediately available wherever allergy immunotherapy injections are administered?

options.text: Oral diphenhydramine (Benadryl)
Epinephrine 1:1000 injectable
Intravenous methylprednisolone
Inhaled albuterol only

explanation: Epinephrine 1:1000 injectable is the sole first-line treatment for anaphylaxis and must always be immediately on hand. AAAAI practice parameters list it as mandatory in any setting administering immunotherapy.

questionText: How often should epinephrine expiration dates and emergency kit inventory be checked and logged?

options.text: Annually
Quarterly
Monthly
Daily before the first injection session

explanation: Monthly checks are the accepted best-practice standard for allergy offices, ensuring epinephrine is never expired when needed and all emergency supplies are present and functional.

questionText: A patient develops diffuse urticaria, hypotension, and wheezing 8 minutes after their injection. What is the FIRST-LINE treatment?

options.text: Oral antihistamine (diphenhydramine)
Intravenous diphenhydramine
Epinephrine 1:1000 intramuscular injection into the lateral thigh
Supplemental oxygen and observation

explanation: Epinephrine IM (lateral thigh) is the only first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. Antihistamines, steroids, and oxygen are adjuncts — not primary treatments. Delay in epinephrine administration is the leading cause of anaphylaxis-related fatalities.

questionText: What is the recommended frequency for anaphylaxis management drills in an allergy office?

options.text: Once per year
Twice per year
Monthly
Only when new staff join the practice

explanation: Published best-practice recommends twice-yearly anaphylaxis drills. Research shows 57% of allergists have never conducted an office rehearsal — a significant patient safety and liability gap.

questionText: Which statement about OSHA bloodborne pathogen requirements in an allergy clinic is TRUE?

options.text: Allergy offices are exempt from OSHA bloodborne pathogen regulations
Allergy clinics must maintain a Sharps Injury Log and record all injuries from contaminated needles or sharps
Only phlebotomy injuries require a Sharps Injury Log entry
Allergy injections do not pose a bloodborne pathogen exposure risk

explanation: OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1030 applies fully to allergy clinics. Staff use lancets (skin testing) and subcutaneous needles (injections) routinely. A Sharps Injury Log must be maintained for all contaminated needlestick injuries.

questionText: How many patient identifiers must appear on an allergen immunotherapy vial label?

options.text: One (patient name only)
Two (e.g., full name AND date of birth)
Three (name, DOB, and medical record number)
None — patient identifiers are not required on vial labels

explanation: Two patient identifiers are required on every vial (AAAAI/ACAAI standard and Joint Commission requirement) to prevent mix-ups. Typically full name and date of birth.

questionText: Which of the following is NOT a required element on an allergen immunotherapy vial label per AAAAI/ACAAI guidelines?

options.text: Beyond-use date
Two patient identifiers
The prescribing physician's NPI number
Required storage temperature

explanation: The five required elements are: (1) two patient identifiers, (2) allergen content and v/v concentration, (3) color code, (4) beyond-use date, and (5) storage temperature. The physician NPI is a billing identifier, not a required vial label element.

questionText: Which of the following IS a required allergen immunotherapy vial label element?

options.text: Manufacturer lot numbers for each component allergen
v/v concentration of the allergen content
Patient insurance information
The practice's emergency phone number

explanation: The v/v concentration tells the injecting clinician the dilution strength, which is critical for dose accuracy. It is one of the five required label elements.

questionText: What is the primary patient safety risk of omitting the color code from an allergen vial label?

options.text: Insurance claims may be denied
Staff could administer the wrong concentration and trigger severe anaphylaxis
The vial cannot legally be stored in the refrigerator
The BUD cannot be calculated without a color code

explanation: Color codes allow staff to instantly identify dilution strength. Without them, a maintenance-level dose could be administered to a patient still in buildup phase, potentially causing life-threatening anaphylaxis.

questionText: Why is the 'beyond-use date' different from the manufacturer's printed expiration date?

options.text: BUDs are assigned only by licensed pharmacists
The BUD reflects the compounded mixture's stability and cannot exceed the earliest component expiry or 1 year from mixing
BUDs apply only to oral medications, not injectables
BUDs are always exactly 6 months shorter than the manufacturer expiry

explanation: When allergens are combined with a diluent, the stability of the mixture is governed by its weakest component. USP 797 §21 defines BUD as the earlier of: the earliest component expiry, or 1 year from the compounding date.

questionText: What storage instruction must appear on every allergen immunotherapy vial label?

options.text: Store at room temperature (20–25°C)
Refrigerate at 2–8°C; do not freeze
Store at 15°C in a controlled cool environment
Storage temperature labeling is optional for in-house compounded vials

explanation: Refrigeration at 2–8°C is mandatory; freezing destroys allergen potency through protein denaturation. The storage temperature requirement is a mandatory label element under AAAAI/ACAAI guidelines.

questionText: When did USP General Chapter 797 Section 21 (governing allergen extract compounding) become effective?

options.text: January 1, 2023
July 1, 2023
November 1, 2023
January 1, 2024

explanation: The revised USP 797, including §21 (the allergen extract exception), took effect November 1, 2023. All allergen extract compounders must comply from that date forward.

questionText: Under USP 797 §21, how frequently must compounding competency be formally re-assessed for active compounders?

options.text: Every 6 months
Annually (every 12 months)
Every 2 years
Only upon initial hire

explanation: USP 797 §21 requires annual re-assessment of all three competency components for every active compounder, regardless of experience level.

questionText: A compounder has not mixed any allergen extracts in 7 months. What must occur before they resume compounding?

options.text: No action needed — their most recent annual competency remains valid
A brief SOP review and supervisor sign-off
Complete re-evaluation of all three components: written quiz, gloved fingertip sampling, and media fill test
Only the media fill test needs to be repeated

explanation: USP 797 §21 includes a 6-month inactivity rule: anyone who has not compounded for more than 6 consecutive months must redo all three competency components from scratch before resuming, regardless of their last annual evaluation date.

questionText: What physician oversight does AAAAI practice parameters require during allergy immunotherapy injection administration?

options.text: A physician must be reachable by phone
A qualified physician must be physically on the premises and immediately available
A physician must personally observe every injection
Physician oversight is only required during the buildup phase

explanation: AAAAI parameters require the physician to be on premises — not just reachable by phone — because anaphylaxis requires immediate physician intervention. Most state medical boards echo this requirement.

questionText: What is the correct refrigerator temperature range for allergen extract storage?

options.text: Room temperature (20–25°C) is acceptable for short-term storage

explanation: The accepted safe range is 2–8°C. Below 2°C risks freezing (destroys potency); above 8°C accelerates protein degradation.

questionText: Why is glycerin used as the diluent for allergen extract vials rather than normal saline alone?

options.text: FDA regulations require glycerin for all allergen injectables
Glycerin provides preservative and stabilizing properties that slow extract degradation
Glycerin is less expensive than normal saline
Glycerin has no clinical benefit and is used only by tradition

explanation: 50% glycerin diluents significantly extend the stability of allergen extracts compared to aqueous diluents, acting as a preservative and reducing proteolytic enzyme activity that degrades allergen proteins over time.

questionText: Which scenario would MOST rapidly reduce allergen extract potency?

options.text: Temperature excursion above 8°C — vial left at room temperature for several hours
Storing vials in a dark refrigerator
Mixing with 50% glycerin diluent
Using sterile normal saline as a diluent

explanation: Temperature excursions above 8°C accelerate protein degradation rapidly. Any vial left at room temperature for extended periods must be considered potentially compromised and reported to the supervising physician.

questionText: A temperature log shows the allergen extract refrigerator reached 12°C overnight. What is the correct response?

options.text: Document the reading and continue using the extracts normally
Quarantine all affected vials, document the full excursion (duration and peak temp), notify the physician, and assess extract integrity before further use
Add ice packs and recheck in 30 minutes; proceed if temperature returns to range
Discard only vials stored for more than 6 months

explanation: Any temperature excursion out of 2–8°C is a critical event. Vials must be quarantined until the physician assesses whether the excursion was long enough to compromise potency. Duration and peak temperature must be documented.

questionText: Why is freezing allergen extract vials NOT recommended even to extend their useful life?

options.text: Freezing is prohibited under AAAAI membership policy
Ice crystal formation during freezing causes protein denaturation and permanent loss of allergen potency
Frozen vials cannot legally be administered to patients per CMS rules
Freezing is permitted for stock concentrates but not for diluted patient vials

explanation: Freezing forms ice crystals that physically disrupt allergen protein structure, irreversibly reducing immunologic potency. The 2–8°C refrigeration requirement maintains protein integrity without ice damage.
